era_context: The Classical Greek world is fragmented following the Battle of Mantinea, with no single power dominating. Philip II of Macedon is consolidating power in the north and will soon emerge as a major player in Greek affairs. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II is in its final years, facing succession challenges. The Roman Republic continues to consolidate power in central Italy. In China, the Warring States period continues with increasing military competition.

events.title: Philip II consolidates power in Macedon
events.description: Philip II of Macedon, having recently assumed power (c. 359–360 BCE), begins a rapid consolidation of Macedonian authority. Philip reorganizes the Macedonian army, introducing new tactics and discipline that will make Macedon a major military power. He also engages in diplomatic maneuvering with Greek city-states and neighboring kingdoms. By 360 BCE, Philip is establishing himself as a significant player in regional politics, though he is not yet a dominant force in broader Greek affairs. His rise will eventually lead to Macedonian conquest of Greece and the creation of the Hellenistic world.

sources.name: Plutarch, Life of Alexander (contains information about Philip)

events.certainty_note: Philip II's consolidation of power is well-attested in later sources, though the exact chronology of events in 360 BCE is not always clear.

events.title: Satraps' Revolt concludes in Persian Empire
events.description: The Satraps' Revolt against Artaxerxes II reaches its conclusion around 360 BCE (exact dating disputed). Multiple provincial governors who had rebelled against the Great King's authority are defeated or submit. The suppression of the revolt demonstrates the continued military power of the Persian Empire, but also reveals the internal instability that will plague the empire in the coming decades. The exact chronology of the revolt is disputed among modern scholars, with some sources placing the peak of the rebellion earlier (c. 366 BCE) and others later (c. 360 BCE).
events.certainty_note: The Satraps' Revolt is well-attested, but its precise chronology is disputed among modern scholars. Different sources place the rebellion's conclusion between 366 and 360 BCE.

events.title: Artaxerxes II Mnemon in final years of reign
events.description: Artaxerxes II Mnemon, who has ruled the Persian Empire since 405 BCE, is now in his final years (he will die in 358 BCE). The aging Great King faces succession challenges and internal instability. By 360 BCE, the question of succession is becoming increasingly pressing, as Artaxerxes II has multiple sons and the succession is not clearly established. The empire remains stable, but the transition of power will be contested.
events.certainty_note: The reign of Artaxerxes II is well-documented, but the exact timing of succession challenges is uncertain.

events.title: Roman expansion in central Italy
events.description: The Roman Republic continues its gradual expansion and consolidation of power in central Italy. By 360 BCE, Rome has secured dominance over the Latin League and is beginning to extend influence into Campania. The internal conflict between patricians and plebeians continues, with the plebeian class gradually gaining political rights. Rome is not yet a major Mediterranean power but is establishing the military and administrative structures that will enable later expansion.
events.certainty_note: Roman history of this period is known primarily through Livy, whose sources are now lost. Specific events of 360 BCE are difficult to verify independently.

events.title: Warring States period in China continues
events.description: During the Warring States period (475–221 BCE), the Chinese world is fragmented into competing kingdoms. By 360 BCE, states such as Qi, Chu, Zhao, and others are engaged in ongoing military and diplomatic competition. The period is marked by increasing military sophistication, administrative development, and philosophical ferment. Specific events of 360 BCE are not well-attested in surviving sources.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for this period are preserved in later compilations. Specific dating of events within the Warring States period is often uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of 360 BCE as a turning point in Greek history is sometimes overstated. While Philip II's consolidation of power is important, his dominance of Greece is not yet apparent in 360 BCE; it will take decades for Macedon to become the dominant Greek power. The Satraps' Revolt's chronology is disputed; some scholars place its conclusion earlier or later than 360 BCE. The rise of Philip II is sometimes portrayed as inevitable in later sources, but in 360 BCE, his future dominance is not yet certain.
historiographic_note: Sources for 360 BCE are primarily Greek and Persian accounts preserved in later compilations. The year 360 BCE is significant primarily as the year when Philip II consolidates power in Macedon, but the full implications of this consolidation are not apparent in contemporary sources. The Satraps' Revolt's chronology is disputed among modern scholars, making the exact status of this rebellion in 360 BCE uncertain. Roman and Chinese sources for this year are less detailed. The year 360 BCE marks a turning point in Greek history, but the broader implications of this turning point are debated among modern scholars.

graph_edges.note: Philip II's consolidation of power leads to Macedonian dominance of Greece at the Battle of Chaeronea
The conclusion of the Satraps' Revolt is followed by succession crisis upon Artaxerxes II's death
Artaxerxes II's final years lead to succession crisis
